heading: What It Signals
body: "Provided that" usually introduces a conditional statement. It marks a condition under which another statement holds.

examples.text: "May go to the party provided that finish homework" has conditional form.
"The lamp is on provided that the switch works" has conditional form.

note: This lesson builds on the earlier lesson on standard conditionals.

heading: Sufficient Condition
body: A **sufficient condition** is a condition that guarantees another statement. In "$P$ provided that $Q$," the phrase after "provided that" gives the sufficient condition.

examples.text: In "May go to the party provided that finish homework," "finish homework" is sufficient for "may go to the party."

heading: Translation Pattern
body: Translate "$P$ provided that $Q$" as $Q \rightarrow P$. Write the statement after "provided that" as the antecedent, then write the other statement as the consequent.

examples.text: "May go to the party provided that finish homework" becomes $H \rightarrow P$.
"May go to the party provided that finish homework" becomes $P \rightarrow H$.

heading: Simple Translations
body: Choose a letter for each simple statement. Then place the provided-that clause on the left of $\rightarrow$.

examples.text: Let $H$ = Finish homework, $P$ = May go to the party. "May go to the party provided that finish homework" becomes $H \rightarrow P$.
Let $K$ = Have the key, $D$ = Open the door. "May open the door provided that have the key" becomes $K \rightarrow D$.
Let $R$ = It rains, $G$ = The game is canceled. "The game is canceled provided that it rains" becomes $G \rightarrow R$.

heading: Do Not Reverse
body: A common mistake is putting the main statement first in symbols because it appears first in English. With "provided that," the phrase after it becomes the antecedent.

examples.text: "The store opens provided that the truck arrives" becomes $T \rightarrow S$.
"The store opens provided that the truck arrives" becomes $S \rightarrow T$.

heading: When It Becomes Both Ways
body: A **biconditional** is a two-way statement. "Provided that" alone gives one direction, but if the context also says the condition is necessary, the full translation becomes a biconditional.

examples.text: "Entry is allowed provided that you have a ticket, and only if you have a ticket" supports $E \leftrightarrow T$.
"Entry is allowed provided that you have a ticket" by itself supports $E \leftrightarrow T$.

note: Look for added wording such as "only if," "if and only if," or a definition that states both directions.

heading: Core Pattern
body: Translate "$P$ provided that $Q$" as $Q \rightarrow P$. Use a biconditional only when the context clearly states both directions.
